metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define (or reference by section) the enriched sidecar field vocabulary the four-pass breakdown display will read — specifically: which fields carry pass-1 pre-filter findings, pass-2 per-finding binary sub-answers, pass-3 threshold values, and pass-4 coaching output — so the UI template and its oracle test can be written without out-of-band negotiation.
findings.finding: The plan promises 'each review's four passes shown including thresholds' (Success Criteria) and 'pass-1..4 detail and thresholds' (AC) but never defines what data vocabulary backs the four-pass breakdown display. The existing `plan_review_result_v1` sidecar (`build_payload` in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py`) stores only post-aggregated findings (with `decision`/`severity`/`priority`/`verification` per finding) — it does NOT store raw pass-1 pre-filter output, pass-2 per-finding binary sub-answers verbatim, the `block_threshold` value used at pass-3, or full pass-4 coaching move templates. The plan defers to the hard-dependency enrichment epic for 'lossless' data but never specifies which enriched fields that epic must expose to enable the four-pass breakdown the UI must render.
findings.location: Success Criteria; Acceptance Criteria (AC line: 'pass-1..4 detail and thresholds'); Scope ('per-ticket audit page rendering')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Data contract' subsection under Approach (or as an interface table) listing the minimal field set the dependency epic must emit for each of the four panels — e.g. `pass1_findings[]`, `pass2_verifications{}`, `pass3_threshold`, `pass4_coaching[]` — even if the exact names are pending the enrichment epic's design, noting them as TBD-but-required so both stories share a named interface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an oracle test (or extend the existing regression suite) asserting that at least the primary existing commands (`rebar review`, `rebar verify`, or equivalent) complete without error after the new config key and subcommand are registered, with `[ui] enabled = false` (default).
findings.finding: Parent AC-3's oracle ('all existing commands behave unchanged') is scope-narrowed by child a3d7: the child only oracle-tests `import rebar` (no web dep) and the `rebar audit serve` disabled path — no child oracle-tests that commands OTHER than `audit serve` (e.g., `rebar review`, `rebar verify`) continue to behave unchanged after the new config key and CLI subcommand are added.
findings.suggested_fix: In child a3d7, extend the disabled-path test to invoke two or three existing CLI commands with the default config and assert zero-exit / no-web-import, covering the 'all existing commands behave unchanged' clause of the parent AC-3 oracle.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in a3d7's scope whether the index page is rendered via a Jinja2 template (preferred, to exercise the full stack) or an inline response, and if the latter, acknowledge that the Jinja2 integration is deferred to ff6f and update the rationale accordingly.
findings.finding: Child a3d7 does not specify whether the index page uses Jinja2 templates, yet child ff6f introduces the templates directory (`src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html`) for the per-ticket page — if a3d7 renders its index page with an inline HTML response string rather than a Jinja2 template, the two children will have inconsistent rendering approaches in the same module, and the templates infrastructure won't be proven end-to-end by a3d7's vertical-slice goal.
findings.location: child a3d7-fadb-e7e9-4e2c: Scope section; child ff6f-b655-4058-4308: Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State how `rebar audit serve` identifies the target ticket (e.g., 'takes a required positional `ticket-id` argument: `rebar audit serve TICKET-123`') so the subcommand's CLI signature is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The `rebar audit serve` subcommand signature does not specify how the target ticket is identified — whether by a positional argument, a `--ticket-id` flag, or a config value. An executor implementing the CLI would have to make an unguided design choice.
findings.location: Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria — 'rebar audit serve' subcommand
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Success Criteria: 'The subcommand accepts a required positional argument `ticket-id` (e.g., `rebar audit serve PROJ-42`) identifying the ticket whose audit trail is rendered.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default listen port (or that it is configurable via `--port`) so the subcommand and its e2e oracle can agree on the address without executor guesswork.
findings.finding: The listen port for the web server is not specified. An executor must pick one arbitrarily, potentially conflicting with other local services or with the e2e test that must connect to it.
findings.location: Approach rationale / Acceptance Criteria — server startup
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Defaults to port 8787 (overridable via `--port`); the e2e test binds to an ephemeral port to avoid conflicts.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (with an oracle) that explicitly verifies navigation across multiple plan- and code-review history rounds — e.g. 'given a ticket with N>1 plan-review rounds, the UI exposes a way to select each round and render that round's four passes.'
findings.finding: History navigation across plan- and code-review rounds is described as a core deliverable in Context and Success Criteria but has no covering acceptance criterion or oracle test.
findings.location: Success Criteria (first bullet) and Acceptance Criteria line 2
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC line 2 or add AC line 4: 'Given a ticket with more than one plan-review round, the UI renders a round-selector and correctly displays each round's pass-1..4 detail. Oracle: end-to-end test that loads round 1 and round 2 and asserts each renders the correct pass content.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name or describe the two child stories inline in the plan (e.g. '(1) subcommand + config wiring, (2) per-ticket audit page rendering') so AC line 1 is self-contained and verifiable.
findings.finding: AC line 1 references 'child stories' that are neither enumerated nor described anywhere in the plan body, making the criterion unverifiable from the plan alone.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria line 1: 'Both child stories are closed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Approach section whether the `nava-rebar[ui]` extra is a new extra that *depends on the same libraries* as the reviewbot extra, or whether the UI is added into the reviewbot extra itself — and update the wording so Approach and Scope/Compatibility are consistent.
findings.finding: Approach says 'REUSING the `reviewbot` extra's already-shipped web stack' (implying the UI lives inside the existing reviewbot installable extra), while Scope and Compatibility introduce a distinct new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra — an implementer cannot tell whether to add to the existing extra or create a new one.
findings.location: Approach section vs. Scope / Compatibility & rollback sections
findings.suggested_fix: Change Approach to: 'reusing the same web-framework libraries (FastAPI + Jinja2) already used by the `reviewbot` extra, but packaged in a new `nava-rebar[ui]` optional extra so the UI remains independently opt-in.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that verifies the server binds exclusively to 127.0.0.1 (loopback) and does not accept connections on any other interface.
findings.finding: Loopback-only binding is described as a security/operational deliverable in both Approach and Scope but is not verified by any acceptance criterion or oracle.
findings.location: Approach section ('bound to loopback only') and Scope IN list; no corresponding AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'The server binds exclusively to 127.0.0.1; connections attempted from a non-loopback address are refused. Oracle: test that the listening socket address is 127.0.0.1 and a connection attempt to the host's external IP is rejected.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the 'optional lazy-imported FastAPI extra' architectural decision (including the rejected static-export alternative) in an ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or named design doc referenced from this plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — an optional, lazy-imported, loopback-only FastAPI extra as the mechanism for adding optional web surfaces to the CLI — but does not specify capturing this decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc. Future contributors have no durable record of why this pattern was chosen over alternatives (static export, separate process, etc.).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a child task or AC item: 'Update CLAUDE.md (or create docs/adr/NNN-optional-ui-extra.md) documenting the optional-extra pattern: lazy import, loopback binding, [ui] extra, and rationale for rejecting static HTML export.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC oracle for history navigation: e.g., 'the end-to-end test requests a prior plan-review round by index and asserts that round's findings are rendered correctly.'
findings.finding: The 'history navigation' goal (navigating across retained plan- and code-review history rounds) is named in Success Criteria but has no matching end-state-observable proof in the Acceptance Criteria — the AC oracle only asserts on a single ticket audit page with pass-1..4 detail, not on round-to-round navigation.
findings.location: Success Criteria (bullet 1) vs. Acceptance Criteria (second bullet, Oracle clause)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the second AC bullet: 'Oracle: a live end-to-end test that (a) starts the server, (b) asserts on the rendered ticket audit page including pass-1..4 detail and thresholds, and (c) requests a prior review round by index and asserts that round's content is rendered, confirming history navigation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Approach rationale that Jinja2 is a net-new dep for the `[ui]` extra (not already in `reviewbot`), and confirm `nava-rebar[ui]` explicitly declares `jinja2` alongside `fastapi` and `uvicorn[standard]`.
findings.finding: The plan's positive rationale claims it is 'REUSING the reviewbot extra's already-shipped web stack,' but Jinja2 — the template engine required for server-side Jinja2 rendering — is absent from the existing `reviewbot` extra (`['fastapi', 'uvicorn[standard]', 'boto3']`). The new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra will need to declare Jinja2 as a net-new dependency. This overstates the reuse and could lead an implementer to omit Jinja2 from the new extra's dep list.
findings.location: Approach rationale section — 'REUSING the reviewbot extra's already-shipped web stack'
findings.suggested_fix: Revise the rationale to: 'Reusing the FastAPI framework pattern already established by the reviewbot extra, with Jinja2 added as a net-new dep in the new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra (reviewbot ships fastapi + uvicorn; the ui extra adds jinja2 for server-side templating).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `jinja2` is explicitly listed as a dependency of the new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra (it is not in the existing `reviewbot` extra), and update the plan's 'reusing the already-shipped web stack' framing to accurately reflect that Jinja2 is a new addition.
findings.finding: The plan claims to REUSE 'the `reviewbot` extra's already-shipped web stack' but simultaneously introduces a new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra. The existing `reviewbot` extra contains only `fastapi`, `uvicorn[standard]`, and `boto3` (pyproject.toml line 93) — it does NOT include `jinja2`. FastAPI's server-side templating (`fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates`) requires `jinja2` as a separate install-time dependency. The plan's capability assertion that it reuses the 'already-shipped' stack for Jinja2 templating is unverified and contradicted by the current extra's declared deps.
findings.location: Approach rationale section; `nava-rebar[ui]` extra; pyproject.toml line 93
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope / Compatibility section, list `jinja2` (and optionally `python-multipart` if forms are ever needed) as explicit deps of the `[ui]` extra in pyproject.toml, e.g.: `ui = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "jinja2"]`. Revise the Approach rationale to say 'reusing FastAPI from the reviewbot extra, adding jinja2 for server-side templates' rather than implying jinja2 is already shipped.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the `nava-rebar[ui]` extra's package list in the plan and explicitly include `jinja2` (or `fastapi[templating]` which bundles it), confirming it is not already transitively available from plain `fastapi`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'FastAPI + Jinja2' server-side rendering as a reuse of the existing reviewbot stack, but Jinja2 is NOT currently installed, NOT declared in any pyproject.toml extra, and NOT used anywhere in the existing codebase (the reviewbot app uses only JSONResponse — no Jinja2Templates). FastAPI does not install Jinja2 automatically (it is an optional dep of fastapi[templating]). The new `[ui]` extra package list is never enumerated in the plan, so Jinja2 may be silently missing at runtime.
findings.location: Approach rationale — 'Server-rendered FastAPI + Jinja2'; Compatibility & rollback — 'its web deps live in a new nava-rebar[ui] extra'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Scope or Compatibility section, add: 'The `[ui]` extra declares `["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "jinja2"]` (Jinja2 is not a transitive of plain `fastapi` and must be listed explicitly).' Correspondingly update `pyproject.toml` when implementing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit access-posture declaration to the plan (e.g., in Approach rationale or a new Security section): state that the `rebar audit serve` endpoint is *intentionally unauthenticated, loopback-only, read-only, single-user*, with rationale — and note that any future change to bind address, auth model, or write actions must revisit this contract.
findings.finding: The plan never formally declares the access posture of the `rebar audit serve` endpoint. It states 'loopback only' and 'read-only' but does not record an explicit access class (e.g., 'public-with-rationale: loopback-only single-user tool, no auth by design'). Under the zero-trust caveat, even a loopback surface has a reduced-blast-radius boundary (other local processes and browser tabs on the same machine can reach localhost), so the posture should be affirmatively declared rather than left implicit.
findings.location: Approach rationale / Compatibility & rollback / Acceptance Criteria (AC line: 'rebar audit serve serves a ticket audit page')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Security posture' paragraph to the Approach rationale: 'The audit server binds exclusively to 127.0.0.1 (loopback) and exposes only read-only GET endpoints. It is intentionally unauthenticated — access class: public-with-rationale (single-user local tool; no sensitive write surface). Any future change that adds write actions, removes the loopback restriction, or introduces multi-user access MUST re-evaluate this posture and add appropriate controls.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate a schema-to-panel field mapping (even as a table or bullet list) specifying which fields from `plan_review_result_v1`, `code_review_result_v1`, and `completion_verifier_fail_v1` (or their enriched successors) are rendered by each panel, and the 'latest result' resolution rule for each (e.g. how the completion panel distinguishes PASS from FAIL).
findings.finding: The plan names three UI panels (plan-review, completion-verification, code-review) backed by three different sidecar schemas (`plan_review_result_v1`, `code_review_result_v1`, `completion_verifier_fail_v1`) but provides no co-located mapping of which schema fields feed which panel elements. The processing rule — what constitutes 'the latest' result for each panel, which schema fields map to 'thresholds,' 'determination,' etc. — is not co-located with the UI spec, leaving the panel-rendering logic ambiguous for an executor.
findings.location: Context section ('latest plan review, the completion-verification result, and the latest associated code review'); Success Criteria ('plan/completion/code panels')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in a3d7's scope (or a shared internal design note) the FastAPI app/router structure, the Jinja2Templates instance location, and the templates directory path that ff6f will consume, so both children integrate without assumption mismatch.
findings.finding: The interface contract between a3d7 and ff6f for the shared `src/rebar/audit/server.py` file is unspecified: a3d7 creates the FastAPI app and index route but does not document what app object, router structure, or Jinja2 environment it will expose for ff6f to extend, creating an implicit shared-state coupling that could cause integration friction.
findings.location: child a3d7-fadb-e7e9-4e2c: Scope section (server.py); child ff6f-b655-4058-4308: Scope section (server.py)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to a3d7's scope: 'The app module exposes a module-level `templates = Jinja2Templates(directory=...)` instance and an `APIRouter` that the per-ticket story can import and extend; the templates directory is `src/rebar/audit/templates/`.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
